name: Close Encounters Scale
description: The Close Encounters Scale is a system for classifying UFO sightings based on the level of interaction between the observer and the UFO or its occupants. Proposed by astronomer and UFO researcher J. Allen Hynek in his 1972 book 'The UFO Experience: A Scientific Inquiry,' the scale originally defined three types of encounters: Close Encounter of the First Kind (CE-I) involving sighting a UFO at close range; Close Encounter of the Second Kind (CE-II) involving physical evidence or effects associated with a UFO; and Close Encounter of the Third Kind (CE-III) involving observation of animate beings, or 'aliens,' in association with a UFO. Later, additional categories were added, most notably Close Encounter of the Fourth Kind (CE-IV), referring to alien abduction experiences, and Close Encounter of the Fifth Kind (CE-V), referring to intentional, two-way communication between humans and extraterrestrials. Proponents argue the scale provides a framework for categorizing and studying UFO phenomena, emphasizing the importance of eyewitness testimony and physical evidence. They see CE-III, IV and V encounters as evidence of extraterrestrial visitation and interaction with humanity. Mainstream science generally dismisses the Close Encounters Scale as lacking empirical validity. The scale is criticized for relying heavily on subjective accounts, which are prone to error, misinterpretation, and fabrication. Skeptics argue that reported effects and alleged abductions can be explained by psychological factors, such as suggestibility, sleep paralysis, and false memories, or by misidentification of conventional objects or phenomena. Furthermore, the lack of verifiable physical evidence for CE-III, IV, and V encounters makes them scientifically untenable.
subtitle: Classifying UFO sightings and interactions

claims.claim: UFO sightings can be classified based on proximity and interaction.
claims.evidence: Eyewitness accounts of UFO sightings and alleged interactions.
claims.criticism: Eyewitness testimony is subjective and prone to error; lacks verifiable physical evidence.

claims.claim: Some UFO encounters involve observation of alien beings.
claims.evidence: Reports of CE-III encounters with descriptions of alien entities.
claims.criticism: Descriptions vary widely; may be influenced by popular culture; lack of corroborating evidence.

claims.claim: Alien abduction is a real phenomenon.
claims.evidence: Personal accounts of CE-IV encounters involving abduction and experimentation.
claims.criticism: Abduction experiences may be explained by sleep paralysis, false memories, or psychological trauma.

keyFigures.name: J. Allen Hynek
keyFigures.role: Originator of the Close Encounters Scale

keyFigures.name: Philip J. Klass
keyFigures.role: UFO skeptic and critic of Hynek's work

mythologyConnections: Fairies and Changelings (alleged alien abduction parallels)
Ancient Astronaut Theory (connection of UFOs to ancient myths)

timeline.event: J. Allen Hynek publishes 'The UFO Experience' introducing the Close Encounters Scale.
The CE-IV category (alien abduction) gains prominence in UFO research.
Continued debate and research surrounding the validity and interpretation of close encounters.

sources.author: J. Allen Hynek
